narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the average for students in our dataset. Your LSAT score of 35.50 is close to the typical 36.70 average, and your middle family income bracket reflects many applicants' backgrounds. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.8% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile in Year 3 of school, where you ranked in the 2nd decile, well below the average 5.53, strongly pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the typical 5.71, added to this concern as the next most influential factor. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success compared to others, and your undergraduate GPA provided a similar positive nudge. Your full-time student status was a minor helpful factor, aligning with the vast majority of applicants, while family income had little sway. Overall, your profile stands out from typical students mainly due to those lower early academic deciles, which outweighed your competitive test scores and GPA. We know this decision may feel disheartening, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in academic pipelines. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law prep. You might also aim to elevate your LSAT further, building on its already positive impact. Keep pushing forward—many have turned similar profiles around with targeted improvements, and your path to law school is still very much within reach.
